food_name: Fish Soup with Bee Hoon (Milky Broth, Sliced White Fish, Rice Vermicelli)
aliases: Sliced fish soup with bee hoon
鱼汤米粉 (Yú Tāng Mǐ Fěn)

english: Sliced fish soup with bee hoon
chinese: 鱼汤米粉 (Yú Tāng Mǐ Fěn)

food_subgroup: Fish broth soup with rice vermicelli (bee hoon); complete hawker meal comprising milky fish bone broth, sliced white fish, rice vermicelli, tomato, and spring onion

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Singapore-style sliced fish soup with bee hoon. Closest parent would be a fish soup or fish broth dish; no exact match available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish soup — closest FoodEx2 parent category for broth-based fish soup dishes; no Singapore-specific bee hoon fish soup code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (fish soup) used as closest defensible parent. No specific code for Singapore milky fish soup with rice vermicelli.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for Singapore fish soup with bee hoon identified.

main_ingredients: sliced white fish (batang / Spanish mackerel or wolf herring)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre bowl of fish soup bee hoon weighs approximately 500g, comprising roughly 280ml milky fish broth, 120g sliced white fish, 130g cooked rice vermicelli, 30g tomato, and a garnish of spring onion and ginger.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Soup Bee Hoon — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB / Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Fish Soup Bee Hoon (milky broth variant). Values represent the composite dish per 100g edible portion. Micronutrients for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (batang fish, rice vermicelli, tomato) where HPB does not report these fields directly; confidence for those three fields is 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (bee hoon) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is assigned based on the GI of rice vermicelli (bee hoon) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The fish, broth, tomato, and spring onion components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect the composite GI. GL per 500g bowl calculated as: GI (58) × available carbohydrate per serving (42.5g from 500g × 8.5g/100g) ÷ 100 = 24.7. This is a composite dish estimate; the dominant carbohydrate source is the bee hoon (~130g cooked per bowl).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~650mg per 500g bowl from fish bone stock, seasoning, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: At 130mg sodium per 100g (650mg per standard 500g bowl), fish soup bee hoon is among the lower-sodium complete hawker meals in Singapore, but patients with hypertension should request no added soy sauce or chilli at the table, as condiment additions can contribute an additional 400–600mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycaemic load ~24.7 per bowl driven by ~130g cooked bee hoon (rice vermicelli, GI 58)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The moderate glycaemic load (~24.7 per 500g bowl) is primarily driven by the bee hoon portion; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request half-portion bee hoon to reduce GL to approximately 12–13, while the high fish protein content (28g per bowl) provides meaningful glycaemic buffering and satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (1.5g per 500g bowl), moderate omega-3 from batang fish, and low energy density (65 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish soup bee hoon is a cardioprotective hawker meal choice: saturated fat is only 1.5g per bowl, cholesterol is moderate at 125mg per bowl, and batang (Spanish mackerel) provides omega-3 fatty acids; patients with cardiovascular disease should choose this dish over higher-fat alternatives such as laksa or nasi lemak, and minimise added condiments to control sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory profile: low saturated fat, fish-derived omega-3, selenium, and vitamin D
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory nutrient profile of fish soup bee hoon — including omega-3 from batang fish, selenium (~75mcg per bowl), vitamin D (~5mcg per bowl), and lycopene from tomato — makes it a suitable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis seeking to reduce systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Soup with Bee Hoon (Milky Broth) is one of Singapore's most iconic 'clean eating' hawker dishes, widely regarded as a nutritious and comforting meal. The dish consists of tender slices of fresh white fish — most commonly batang (Spanish mackerel) or wolf herring (parang) — simmered or poached in a milky fish bone broth made by vigorously boiling fish bones, head, ginger, and sometimes soybeans or anchovies. The milky white colour of the broth results from the emulsification of fish oils and collagen during high-heat boiling, not from added cream; however, some hawker stalls add a small amount of evaporated milk for extra richness. The dish is served with delicate rice vermicelli (bee hoon), sliced tomato, and a garnish of spring onion and ginger. At 65 kcal per 100g and approximately 325 kcal per standard 500g bowl, it is one of the lowest-calorie complete hawker meals available. Each bowl provides approximately 28g of high-quality fish protein and only 1.5g of saturated fat, making it suitable for patients managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Sodium is moderate at approximately 650mg per bowl — among the lower values for complete hawker meals in Singapore. The primary glycaemic consideration is the bee hoon (GI 58, medium), which generates a glycaemic load of approximately 24.7 per bowl; requesting half-portion bee hoon reduces GL to approximately 12–13. The dish is halal-certified at most hawker stalls and is gluten-free. It is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, frequently recommended by dietitians and clinicians as a reference 'healthy hawker meal' for dietary counselling.
common_user_queries: sliced fish soup bee hoon glycemic index

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes). GI value for rice vermicelli (bee hoon) is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Three micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (batang fish, rice vermicelli, tomato) where HPB does not report these fields directly; confidence for those three fields is 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology uses closest defensible parent codes with honest gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singapore hawker dish (milky fish soup with bee hoon) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this dish type per HPB Singapore data. Copper (0.04mg/100g), manganese (0.08mg/100g), and vitamin K (5.5mcg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for batang fish, cooked rice vermicelli, and tomato; confidence 0.9 for these three fields. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (bee hoon) GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI is dominated by the bee hoon carbohydrate fraction. Two portions are documented in the source record: 500g (primary, verified) and 450g (secondary, enriched_json); the 500g bowl is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The milky broth variant may have slightly higher fat and energy than the clear broth variant; values reflect the milky broth preparation. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible parent (fish soup); no Singapore-specific bee hoon fish soup code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
